title: Red Wine on Wool
stainChemistry: Tannins are polyphenolic plant compounds (from tea, coffee, red wine, fruit juices) that bond to fibers through hydrogen bonding and π-stacking interactions with cellulose and protein fibers. Fresh tannin stains are removable with mild detergent. Aged stains oxidize and darken: the brown color is caused by oxidation products. Hydrogen peroxide 6% breaks the double bonds in the oxidized tannin molecules, reversing the browning. Sodium hydrosulfite provides a reducing alternative for fibers that cannot tolerate bleaching. On wool (protein fiber): oxidizing agents like hydrogen peroxide can weaken wool keratin — sodium hydrosulfite is the safer reducing treatment.
whyThisWorks: Red wine tannins bond to wool through hydrogen bonding and π-stacking. Wool is a protein fiber, so oxidizing bleach (peroxide) carries risk of fiber weakening — sodium hydrosulfite (reducing agent) attacks tannin pigments without damaging the keratin structure. Tannin Formula (acidic) first breaks the initial tannin-fiber bonds. NSD flushes residue. Acetic acid with fiber conditioner restores the hand and sheen that wool can lose during treatment.
defaultAssumption: Treat as unknown age / possibly dried.

steps: 1. Apply Tannin Formula (acidic tannin spotter) carefully and tamp very gently. Dwell 3–5 minutes (shorter than cotton — use lighter touch on wool). The acidic spotter breaks tannin bonds without attacking the fiber. Monitor closely.

2. Apply sodium hydrosulfite (reducing agent — safe for wool and silk). Dwell 5–10 minutes. This reducing agent attacks tannin pigments without damaging protein fibers like wool and silk — unlike oxidizing agents which can weaken these fabrics. Flush when the color lifts.
3. Flush with NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) to remove all chemical residue and loosened tannin. Dwell 3–5 minutes. Use cool water only. On wool, avoid any agitation that could felt the fiber.

4. Apply 28% Acetic acid (diluted 1:10) with fiber conditioner to neutralize and restore the fiber. Dwell 1–2 minutes. Follow with a fiber conditioner rinse to restore the hand and sheen that protein fibers can lose during treatment. Air dry flat.
products: Sodium hydrosulfite (reducing agent — safe for wool and silk)
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold — heat accelerates tannin oxidation and sets color permanently. On wool, heat also causes felting. Never use warm or hot water at any stage.
warnings: Using hot water — accelerates oxidation and sets tannin color faster, and felts wool fiber.
Applying hydrogen peroxide to colored wool without testing — peroxide bleaches fiber color along with the stain.
Oversaturation with any oxidizer — can cause yellowing and fiber weakening.
Not rinsing completely — residual reducing or oxidizing agents continue to work and weaken fiber.
NEVER use enzyme cleaners on wool — digest the protein fiber.
NEVER use bleach — destroys wool fiber structure.

steps: 1. Blot gently the stain with a clean white cloth to soak up as much as you can.
2. Mix 1 tbsp Dawn dish soap with 1 cup cold water. Apply the solution and let it sit for 5–10 minutes.
3. Blot gently with a clean white cloth. Repeat until the color fades.
4. Rinse gently with cold water to remove all cleaning solution.
5. Lay flat on a towel to air dry — no heat, no wringing.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: If the stain persists after gentle treatment, take it to a professional cleaner who specializes in wool. Do not attempt stronger chemicals at home — wool fibers are easily damaged.
warnings: Test on an inside seam or hidden area first.
Never use bleach, enzyme cleaners, or hot water on wool.
Never wring or twist wool — gently press out moisture between towels.

neverDo: Never use hot water — sets tannin color and felts wool simultaneously.
Never use enzyme cleaners — digest the wool protein fiber.
Never use bleach — destroys wool fiber structure.
Never wring or aggressively agitate — felts wool permanently.
Never use ammonia or alkaline agents — permanently darkens tannin stains.
fiberSensitivities: Wool (keratin, protein fiber) — enzyme cleaners attack it the same way they attack stains.
Wool felts with heat and mechanical agitation — always handle cold and gently.
Mohair is especially vulnerable to oxidizers — avoid peroxide entirely on mohair.
Wool dyes are variable — always test any product on a hidden area first.

escalation.when: If peroxide bleaching reaches a plateau, the tannin has likely cross-linked with the fiber polymer. Try sequential treating: alternate between peroxide (if colorfast) and leveling agent. For extreme cases (wine on white wool), recommend professional-grade tannin-specific chemistry or dye consultation.
escalation.whatToTell: This was a tricky one — wine on wool is one of the more challenging stains. Wine stains darken with air exposure and set deeper with time and heat. Results depend on how long the stain had been set before treatment — some improvement may continue over the next few days.
escalation.specialistType: wet cleaner specializing in delicate wool garments

contextQuestions: How old is the stain — was it treated immediately or has it been sitting?
Has any product already been applied (salt, club soda, enzyme cleaner)?
Is this a fine gauge wool knit or woven wool fabric?
Has the garment been placed in the dryer or pressed since the stain occurred?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Fresh wine on wool responds reasonably well to cold water and dish soap. Any heat exposure (dryer, iron) dramatically reduces recovery odds. Enzyme cleaner applied previously may have caused wool fiber damage that limits recovery. Fine-gauge knitwear requires shorter dwell times and extremely gentle handling to prevent felting.
